title: Identification of Catechin Oligomers from Apple (Malus pumila cv. Fuji) in Matrix-assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-of-flight Mass Spectrometry and Fast-atom Bombardment Mass Spectrometry
abstract:
Identiﬁcation of Catechin Oligomers from
Apple (Malus pumila cv. Fuji) in
Matrix-assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization
Time-of-ﬂight Mass Spectrometry and Fast-atom
Bombardment Mass Spectrometry
Mayumi Ohnishi-Kameyama,1* Akio Yanagida,2 Tomomasa Kanda2 and Tadahiro Nagata1
1National Food Research Institute, Ministry of Agriculature, Forestry and Fisheries, Tsukuba, Ibaraki 305 Japan
2Institute for Production Research and Development, Nikka Whisky Distilling Co., Ltd., Kashiwa, Chiba 277 Japan
Molecular size information for polymerized catechin larger than the decamer in unripe apple was obtained by
matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-ﬂight mass spectrometry and by fast-atom bombardment
mass spectrometry. Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time of ﬂight mass spectrometry provided
evidence for the pentadecamer using trans-3-indoleacrylic acid as the matrix in the presence of silver ion. Even
in the absence of silver ion, the dodecamer and undecamer were observed in the positive- and negative-ion
modes, respectively. Fast-atom bombardment mass spectrometry also afforded evidence for the undecamer in
both positive- and negative-ion modes. © 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Received 30 October 1996; Revised 24 November 1996; Accepted 24 November 1996
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 11, 31–36 (1997)
No. of Figures: 8
No. of Tables: 2
No. of Refs: 11
The ﬂavonoid, catechin and related compounds occur
widely in plants as secondary metabolites from the
ﬂavonoid biosynthesis system. The condensed tannin
(CT), made up of polymerized catechins (Fig. 1), has
various physiological activities, such as antioxidant,1,2
anti-tumor,3,4anti-cariogen,5
anti-mutagenesis5
and
anti-allergy5 that are more pronounced than the activ-
ities of intact catechin and of the dimers. It is also
reported that the different plant-derived CTs give
different thresholds of activities for survival, growth
and food consumption of larvae of cabbage looper
(Trichoplusia ni).6 It remains obscure whether these
activities increase with an increasing number of cate-
chin units, because the assays have been performed
using catechin with a varying degree of polymerization.
This may be attributed to the difﬁculty in determining
accurate values from the range of molecular weights
obtained using size-exclusion chromatography and to
the difﬁculty in isolating single components. The molec-
ular weights of CTs, up to the pentamer, were reported
on the basis of individual molecular ion species by fast-
atom bombardment mass spectrometry (FAB-MS)7–9
and electrospray ionization mass spectrometry.10 Field
desorption mass spectrometry generated the molecular
weight of methylated hexameric catechin.8 However,
catechin oligomers with molecular weights greater than
2000 have not yet been detected successfully in mass
spectrometry (Table 1).
Apples contain a large amount of CT, especially in
unripe fruit (more than ten times that of ripe fruit).5
The apple condensed tannin (ACT), with varying
degree of polymerization, was puriﬁed from unripe
fruits and the molecular weights of the components
in ACT were characterized by matrix-assisted laser
desorption/ionization time-of-ﬂight mass spectrometry
(MALDI-TOFMS) and by FAB-MS. This is the ﬁrst
report of such highly polymerized catechins, obtained
from a natural source, being detected as heterogeneous
samples by mass spectrometric analysis.
EXPERIMENTAL SECTION
Instrumentation
MALDI-TOF mass spectra were acquired on a
REFLEX II (Bruker-Franzen Analytik, Bremen, Ger-
many) instrument, in which samples were irradiated
with a nitrogen laser (wavelength 337 nm; 3 ns pulse)
under high vacuum. The reﬂection mode of operation

tion. Spectra obtained in the linear mode were meas-
ured using 20 kV ion acceleration without postaccelera-
tion. All spectra were recorded with a detector voltage
of 1.6 kV, and were the averaged result of at least 100
laser shots. Laser intensity was variable, providing
maximum ﬂexibility in collecting spectra. The singly
charged molecular ions of insulin and angiotensin II
were used as external standards and a mass calibration
procedure was employed prior to the analysis of each
sample.
FAB-MS used a reverse-geometry (BE) double-
focusing instrument (JMS SX102A, JEOL, Tokyo,
Japan) mass spectrometer equipped with a FAB gun
that produces a 6 keV xenon beam. A resolving power
of 3000 was employed with an ion accleration potential
of 6 to 10 kV. The spectra were obtained by scanning up
to m/z 4000 using a DA 7000 data system. The mass
scale was calibrated with Ultramark 1621 (PCR Inc.,

Gainesville, Fl, USA) or Csl. Metastable ion decom-

Puriﬁcation. The apple condensed tannin (ACT) was
obtained as follows. Unripe apple fruits (the average
weight of individual fruits was about 10 g) of the
cultivar Fuji (Malus pumila cv. Fuji) were harvested in
June at Hirosaki in Japan. These fruits (3 kg) were
homogenized in the presence of potassium pyrosulﬁte
(0.1% w/w), and the homogenate kept at 4 °C for 24 h.
The juice obtained by pressing the fruit was collected
and clariﬁed by centrifugation at 3500 g and ﬁltered
through a glass ﬁlter. The ﬁltrate (clariﬁed juice, 1.8 L)
was applied to a Sepabeads SP-850 (Mitsubishi Kasei,
Tokyo, Japan) column (25 mm i.d. × 285 mm). The
crude apple polyphenol fraction was obtained as an
80% ethanolic eluent (200 mL) after washing the
column with distilled water (300 mL) to remove water-
soluble components. Ethanol was removed from the
eluent in vacuo to obtain 65 mL concentrated crude
apple polyphenol fraction, and a portion (25 mL) of this
fraction was loaded on a TSKgel toyopearl HW-40EC
(TOSOH, Tokyo, Japan) column (25 mm i.d. × 285
mm). This column was washed with distilled water (200
mL), then phenolic compounds were eluted with 40%
aqueous ethanol (250 mL) and 60% aqueous acetone
(100 mL), and fractions (25 mL) were collected. The
earlier material, eluted with 40% ethanol, contained
mainly monomeric catechins, dimeric catechins and
phloretin glycosides, and was further puriﬁed using
Sep-pak C18 ENV (Waters Co., Milford, MA, USA) to
eliminate phloretin glycosides. The resulting solution
and the latter 60% aqueous acetone eluate (containing
mainly oligometic catechins) were mixed, and are
described as ACT in the subsequent experiments. This
Figure 4. MALDI mass spectrum of ACT in the presence of alkali ions (Li+, Na+, K+).
Figure 5. MALDI mass spectrum of ACT in the presence of silver ion.
CATECHIN OLIGOMERS IN MALDI-TOFMS AND FAB-MS
ACT solution was lyophilized after evaporation of
organic solvents. ACT showed the characteristic proﬁle
of catechin in the UV spectrum (absorbance 0.134 at
λmax 280.0 nm (10 mg/L in 50% MeOH)).
3,5-Dimethoxy-4-hydroxycinnamic
acid (sinapinic acid, SA, Tokyo Kasei Kogyo Co. Ltd,
Tokyo, Japan) was recrystallized from methanol. The
other matrices, such as α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic
acid (CCA, Sigma, St. Louis, MO, USA), trans-
3-indoleacrylic acid (IAA, Sigma), 9-nitroanthracene
(9NA, Aldrich Japan, Tokyo), 5-chlorosalycilic acid
(5SCA, Sigma), 2-(4-hydroxyphenylazo) benzoic acid
(HABA, Aldrich), 1,8,9-anthracenetriol dithranol,
Aldrich) and 2,5-dihydroxybezoic acid (gentisic acid,
DHB, Sigma), were used without puriﬁcation. The
concentration of each matrix was 10 g/L in the
appropriate solvent. ACT was dissolved in acetone (500
mg/L) and mixed with a matrix solution (1:1 v/v). The
mixture (ca. 1 µL) was put on a stainless steel target and
crystallized at room temperature. The silver salt of
triﬂuoroacetic acid (Aldrich) and lithium trimethane-
sulfonate (Aldrich) were each dissolved in acetone (1
mM). The concentrations of alkali halides, that were
FAB-MS. The same volume (1 µL) of ACT (50 g/L in
methanol) and ‘magic bullet’ (dithiothreiotol
dithioerythritol = 5:1 Tokyo Kasei Kogyo) were mixed
on a stainless steel probe tip used as the FAB target.
The silver salt of triﬂuoroacetic acid and lithium
trimethanesulfonate were each dissolved in methanol
Chemicals were purchased from Nacalai Tesque
(Kyoto, Japan) where not otherwise mentioned.
The MALDI-TOF spectrum of ACT in IAA showed a
series of peaks separated by 288 u, the incremental
mass for a catechin unit, and extending up to the
undecamer in the negative-ion mode (Fig. 2). The
expected molecular weights from intact ACT are given
by 290 + 288n, where n is the degree of polymerization
of catechin. In the positive-ion mode, no protonated
molecular ions were observed. Instead, two series of
[M + alkali metal]+ ion species, viz. sodium and potas-
sium adduct ions ([M + Na]+ and [M + K]+), were
detected (Fig. 3). These ion species were conﬁrmed by
the production of another series, in this case lithium
addition ions [M + Li]+, when equimolar amounts of
LiCl, NaCl and KCl were added to ACT. The three
series of pseudomolecular ion peaks were observed
with almost equal intensities as shown in Fig. 4.
When silver triﬂuoroacetic acid solution (5 µL) was
mixed with IAA (5 µL) and ACT solution (10 µL), the
alkali metal ion adduct peaks converged and were
replaced by silver ion adducts. This resulted in the
[M + Ag]+ ions having a higher degree of polymeriza-
tion, up to the pentadecamer (Fig. 5). Similar results
could not be achieved with the addition of lithium
In the reﬂectron mode, superior MALDI spectra,
with higher resolution, could be recorded in the
positive- and negative-ion modes. However, the largest
oligomer that was observed was the nonamer (Fig. 6),
i.e. three units smaller than the results obtained in
linear mode (Fig. 3). The poor intensity in the high mass
region was attributed to the lability of the catechin
linkage in the reﬂectron mode because of the post-
Other matrices, such as SA and CCA, were unable to
produce molecular ion peaks at all. Even 9-NA, 5CSA,
HABA and dithranol, which are effective matrices for
the ionization of oligomeric materials, could not yield
ions. DHB afforded molecular ions up to the decamer,
but a higher laser power was needed for ionization than
was the case for IAA, both in the positive- and the
Experiments have shown that very low molecular
weight monomeric catechins are not suitable for
MALDI-TOFMS analysis in matrices other than CCA
and DHB (Table 2), and that dimeric catechins are
unsuitable in matrices other than IAA, CCA, and SA
(Table 2). The molecular ions [M + H]+ of catechin
Figure 6. MALDI mass spectrum of ACT in positive-ion reﬂectron mode.
CATECHIN OLIGOMERS IN MALDI-TOFMS AND FAB-MS
Table 2. Summary of matrices investigated in MALDI-TOF.
Molecular ions or related ions detected (O), not
(Fig. 1, [2S,3R]-2-[3,4-dihydroxyphenyl]-3,4-dihydro-
1[2H]-benzopyran-3,5,7-triol) and epicatechin (Fig. 1,
[2R, 3R] - 2 - [3, 4 - dihydroxyphenyl] - 3, 4 - d ihydro - 1[2H] -
benzopyran-3,5,7-triol) were observed at m/z 291 in the
It has been reported that the magic bullet matrix is
superior to glycerol as a matrix from which to record
the FAB-MS spectra of oligomeric catechins, and that
higher mass fragment ions were more intense in the
negative-ion mode than in the positive-ion mode.9
Certainly, the magic bullet matrix yielded a better
spectra than glycerol. However, in our case, the
undecamer was observed in the positive-ion mode as
well as the negative-ion mode as shown in Figs. 7
The addition of alkali-or silver ions provided bene-
ﬁcial effects in MALDI, whereas in FAB-MS it did not
give encouraging results. Alkali ions made the corre-
sponding addition ions, but with low intensities; silver
ions yielded protonated molecular ions, with less
intensity than in the absence of silver, rather than
molecular ions by silver ion adduction.
The spectrum of ACT (a mixture of catechins of
varying degrees of polymerization) consisted of molec-
ular ions of each of the components and the corre-
sponding fragment ions. Linked-scan analysis was
shown to be a powerful method for the acquisition of
knowledge about interﬂavanoid linkage types. A
linked-scan spectrum at a constant B/E from the trimer
showed the daughter ions at m/z 289, 577 and 579 in the
positive-ion mode. The m/z 577 ion peak resulted from
a carbonium ion at C4 of the middle ﬂavanoid unit,
which is a characteristic fragment ion for a linear-linked
catechin trimer.7 In the negative-ion mode, daughter
ions were observed at m/z 405, 575, 577 and 713; these
are believed to originate via quinone methide mecha-
nisms.7 The m/z 405 and 575 ion peaks indicate that the
linkage type of the trimer is linear. These results
suggested that ACT includes a linear-linked catechin
As well as qualitative measurements, quantitative
measurement is also important for the characterization
of a catechin oligomers mixture. The spectra contain
ions at m/z 290 + 288n, which are interpreted as being
the molecular ions of the (n + 1)-mers. From linked-
scanning at constant B/E results in FAB mass spectrom-
etry, it is believed that a proportion of the n-mer peak
originates from the (n + 1)-mer. However, the pure
dimer showed no monomer peak in the MALDI TOF
mass spectrum and only a low intensity (less than 20%)
of the dimer peak in the FAB mass spectrum. There-
fore, the distribution pattern of catechin oligomers in
the mass spectra does, more or less, reﬂect the
quantities of catechin oligomers present in ACT, taking
into account the differences of the ionization efﬁciency
among the oligomers. Similar arguments were used in
the approximate quantiﬁcation of a catechin oligomer
mixture from the FAB spectrum.11
MALDI-TOF mass spectra recorded in IAA matrix
and FAB mass spectra recorded using the magic bullet
matrix enabled the molecular weight determination of
different monomer untils in catechin oligomers without
further individual isolation. The addition of silver ions
allowed the deﬁnite detection of each component in
MALDI-TOFMS. Furthermore, the sequential order of
a particular oligomer, chosen from the oligomer mix-
ture, was clariﬁed by the use of FAB-MS linked scan
spectra. Making use of mass spectrometry gives each
degree of catechin polymerization an accurate molec-
ular weight, and will lead to the elucidation of
the differences in physiological activities between
We gratefully acknowledge helpful discussions with Mr. T. Nirasawa
on several points in the paper.
